text: The surgeon begins laparoscopic fundoplication by addressing the short gastric vessels, which can aid in accessing the angle of His, especially in obese patients with a hiatus hernia and GERD.

The surgeon applies medial traction to the gastric fundus while using a harmonic scalpel to separate the omentum and clear the short gastric vessels. The hiatus hernia is visible in the left upper corner of the screen.
The surgeon separates the left crus and the adjacent peritoneum, revealing the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The spleen is clearly visible in the surgical field.
And now this is the right side.
The surgeon has dissected the pars flaccida, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon opens the pars fascia, revealing the caudate lobe of the liver. The vena cava is located just below the caudate lobe, requiring careful manipulation to avoid overshooting. A fan retractor is used to maintain exposure, as Nathassan's retractor was not functional. The right crus is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a harmonic scalpel, reaching up to the right crus. The assistant provides traction over the stomach to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the right crus, with visible anterior vagus nerve embedded into the esophagus. Entrolateral and entromedial traction is applied to the fundus, revealing the esophagus and continuing dissection near the left crus.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization, revealing a hernia sac that enters the posterior mediastinum as the stomach is released. The hernia sac is clearly visible, and continuous traction is applied to manage its position.

The surgeon performs posterior mobilization of the esophagus, creating a window while ensuring the vagus nerve remains adjacent to the esophagus. The procedure is conducted without the use of a sling.
A window is created, and a grasper is carefully maneuvered from the right to the left side, ensuring it does not enter the mediastinum or damage the left crus.

The surgeon continues the posterior mobilization of the esophagus within the posterior mediastinum, creating a clear space through which the stomach can be maneuvered. The visual field shows the esophagus being carefully separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon directly extracts the stomach from the surgical field, preparing for the subsequent fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the anterior wall of the fundus of the stomach, ensuring it is not pulled excessively. The fundus is smoothly moved from the left side to the right, demonstrating a floppy fundus with a visible shoe shine effect.

The surgeon performs crural approximation by ligating the second and third knots using a tumble square knot technique, with the Maryland instrument sliding the knot while maintaining the long limb of the needle straight.

The surgeon performs the final step of placation by suturing the stomach to itself, ensuring the esophagus is not included in the first bite. Subsequent sutures sandwich the esophagus between two layers of the stomach, requiring careful technique.
The surgeon takes a full-thickness bite of the stomach tissue, ensuring that only the muscular layer of the esophagus is pierced with the needle to avoid perforation complications.
